---
title: "Stan code — Vevea-Hedges selection model"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{stan-vevea-hedges}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Model description

The Vevea-Hedges (1995) selection model adjusts for publication bias by explicitly modelling the probability that a study is published as a function of its $p$-value. Studies in low-significance bins (high $p$-values) are underrepresented in the literature; the model corrects for this by upweighting their contribution to the likelihood.

## Mathematical specification

**$p$-value bins:**

The $p$-value range $[0, 1]$ is divided into intervals $c_0 = 0 < c_1 < \ldots < c_J = 1$. Typical bin boundaries are $\{0.025, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 1.0\}$.

**Selection weights:**

$$
w_j = P(\text{published} \mid p_i \in (c_{j-1}, c_j])
$$

The weights $w_j$ are constrained to $[0, 1]$ and are typically set so $w_1 = 1$ (studies with $p < 0.025$ are always published).

**Weighted likelihood:**

$$
L(\mu, \tau, \mathbf{w} \mid \mathbf{y}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \frac{w_{b(i)} \cdot p(y_i \mid \mu, \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{J} w_j \cdot P(p_i \in (c_{j-1}, c_j] \mid \mu, \tau)}
$$

where $b(i)$ is the bin of study $i$'s $p$-value and $P(p_i \in (c_{j-1}, c_j] \mid \mu, \tau)$ is the probability of falling in each bin under the model.

**Priors:**

$$
\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5), \qquad w_j \sim \text{Beta}(1,\, 1) \text{ for } j > 1
$$

## Stan code

```stan
data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  int<lower=1> J;
  vector[N] y;
  vector<lower=0>[N] se;
  array[N] int<lower=1> bin;        // p-value bin for each study
  matrix[N, J] bin_probs;           // P(p in bin j | mu, tau) for each study
}

parameters {
  real mu;
  real<lower=0> tau;
  simplex[J] w_raw;
}

transformed parameters {
  vector[J] w = w_raw;
  w[1] = 1.0;
}

model {
  target += normal_lpdf(mu  | 0, 1);
  target += cauchy_lpdf(tau | 0, 0.5);
  for (j in 2:J) {
    target += beta_lpdf(w[j] | 1, 1);
  }

  for (i in 1:N) {
    real denom = dot_product(w, bin_probs[i]);
    target += log(w[bin[i]]) + normal_lpdf(y[i] | mu, sqrt(square(se[i]) + square(tau)))
            - log(denom);
  }
}

generated quantities {
  real b_Intercept = mu;
}
```

## How bayesma calls this model

```r
bayesma(
  data,
  model_type       = "selection_weight",
  p_cutoffs        = c(0.025, 0.05, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 1.0),
  selection_priors = list(w2 = beta(1, 1), w3 = beta(1, 1))
)
```

`bin_probs` is computed internally by `bayesma_stan_data()` using the normal CDF evaluated at the bin boundaries.

## Parameterisation notes

The normalising denominator in the weighted likelihood is the key computational component. It ensures the model correctly accounts for the fact that only published studies are observed.

The $w_j$ parameterisation treats the first bin (most significant) as the reference with $w_1 = 1$. Weaker significance bins have $w_j \leq 1$ by construction.

## Known sampling difficulties

The normalising denominator $\sum_j w_j P(\ldots)$ depends on both $\mu$ and $\tau$, creating a complex likelihood surface. With many bins and small $k$, the $w_j$ posterior is multimodal. Increasing `adapt_delta` to 0.99 and using `iter_warmup = 2000` is recommended.
